curriculum.subject: Physics
curriculum.gradeLevel: Class XI / Class XII / 12th Grade / Science
curriculum.overallDescription: 19 units, 28 chapters, and 0 topics extracted through raw extraction, document-structure hierarchy building, node enrichment, normalized teaching blocks, validation, and statistics generation.
coreObjectives: Identify need for measurement and distinguish between fundamental and derived units within the SI system.
Apply rules for significant figures and determine uncertainty in experimental results.
Perform dimensional analysis to verify equations and derive relationships among physical quantities.
Describe motion in a straight line using elementary differentiation and integration techniques.
Interpret position-time and velocity-time graphs to analyze uniform and non-uniform motion.
Derive and apply relations for uniformly accelerated motion graphically and through calculus.
Perform addition, subtraction, multiplication by scalars, and resolve vectors into rectangular components in a plane.
Calculate scalar and vector products to solve physical problems involving directionality.
Analyze cases of uniform velocity and acceleration including projectile motion and uniform circular motion.
Explain intuitive concepts of force, inertia, and apply Newton's three laws to various scenarios.
Apply the law of conservation of linear momentum and analyze collisions using impulse-momentum theorem.
Analyze equilibrium conditions for concurrent forces and dynamics involving static/kinetic friction.

practicals.title: To measure diameter of a small spherical/cylindrical body and to measure internal diameter and depth of a given beaker/calorimeter using Vernier Callipers and hence find its volume.
To measure diameter of a given wire and thickness of a given sheet using screw gauge.
To determine volume of an irregular lamina using screw gauge.
To determine radius of curvature of a given spherical surface by a spherometer.
To determine the mass of two different objects using a beam balance.
To find the weight of a given body using parallelogram law of vectors.
Using a simple pendulum, plot its graph and use it to find the effective length of second's pendulum.
To study variation of time period of a simple pendulum of a given length by taking bobs of same size but different masses and interpret the result.
To study the relationship between force of limiting friction and normal reaction and to find the co- efficient of friction between a block and a horizontal surface.

To find the downward force, along an inclined plane, acting on a roller due to gravitational pull of the earth and study its relationship with the angle of inclination θ by plotting graph between force and Sinθ.
To determine Young's modulus of elasticity of the material of a given wire.
To find the force constant of a helical spring by plotting a graph between load and extension.
To study the variation in volume with pressure for a sample of air at constant temperature by plotting graphs between P and V, and between P and 1/V.
practicals.unit_name: Behaviour of Perfect Gases and Kinetic Theory of Gases

practicals.title: To determine the surface tension of water by capillary rise method.
To determine the coefficient of viscosity of a given viscous liquid by measuring terminal velocity of a given spherical body.
To study the relationship between the temperature of a hot body and time by plotting a cooling curve.
To determine specific heat capacity of a given solid by method of mixtures.
To study the relation between frequency and length of a given wire under constant tension using sonometer.
To study the relation between the length of a given wire and tension for constant frequency using sonometer.
To find the speed of sound in air at room temperature using a resonance tube by two resonance positions.
To determine resistivity of two / three wires by plotting a graph for potential difference versus current.
To find resistance of a given wire / standard resistor using metre bridge.
To verify the laws of combination (series) of resistances using a metre bridge OR To verify the laws of combination (parallel) of resistances using a metre bridge.
To determine resistance of a galvanometer by half-deflection method and to find its figure of merit.

To convert the given galvanometer (of known resistance and figure of merit) into a voltmeter of desired range and to verify the same OR To convert the given galvanometer (of known resistance and figure of merit) into an ammeter of desired range and to verify the same.
To find the frequency of AC mains with a sonometer.
To find the value of v for different values of u in case of a concave mirror and to find the focal length.
To find the focal length of a convex mirror, using a convex lens.
To find the focal length of a convex lens by plotting graphs between u and v or between 1/u and 1/v.
To find the focal length of a concave lens, using a convex lens.
To determine angle of minimum deviation for a given prism by plotting a graph between angle of incidence and angle of deviation.
To determine refractive index of a glass slab using a travelling microscope.
To find the refractive index of a liquid using convex lens and plane mirror.
To find the refractive index of a liquid using a concave mirror and a plane mirror.
To draw the I-V characteristic curve for a p-n junction diode in forward and reverse bias.
To determine the resistance per cm of a given wire by plotting a graph between voltage and current.
To verify the laws of combination (series/parallel combination) of resistances by Ohm's law.
To find the resistance of a given wire / standard resistor using a meter bridge.
To determine the resistance of a galvanometer by half deflection method.
To identify a resistor, capacitor, inductor and diode from a mixed collection of such items.

To observe the difference between (i) a convex lens and a concave lens (ii) a convex mirror and a concave mirror and to estimate the likely difference between the power of two given convex /concave lenses.
To design an inductor coil and to know the effect of (i) change in the number of turns (ii) Introduction of ferromagnetic material as its core material on the inductance of the coil.
practicals.unit_name: Unit-IV Electromagnetic Induction and Alternating Currents

practicals.title: To design a (i) step up (ii) step down transformer on a given core and know the relation between its input and output voltages.
practicals.unit_name: Unit-IV Electromagnetic Induction and Alternating Currents

activities.title: To make a paper scale of given least count, e.g., 0.2cm, 0.5 cm.
To determine mass of a given body using a metre scale by principle of moments.
To plot a graph for a given set of data, with proper choice of scales and error bars.
To measure the force of limiting friction for rolling of a roller on a horizontal plane.
To study the variation in range of a projectile with angle of projection.
To study the conservation of energy of a ball rolling down on an inclined plane (using a double inclined plane).
To study dissipation of energy of a simple pendulum by plotting a graph between square of amplitude and time.
To observe change of state and plot a cooling curve for molten wax.
To observe and explain the effect of heating on a bi-metallic strip.
To note the change in level of liquid in a container on heating and interpret the observations.
To study the effect of detergent on surface tension of water by observing capillary rise.
To study the factors affecting the rate of loss of heat of a liquid.
To study the effect of load on depression of a suitably clamped metre scale loaded at (i) its end (ii) in the middle.
To observe the decrease in pressure with increase in velocity of a fluid.
To measure the resistance and impedance of an inductor with or without iron core.
To measure resistance, voltage (AC/DC), current (AC) and check continuity of a given circuit using multimeter.
To assemble a household circuit comprising three bulbs, three (on/off) switches, a fuse and a power source.
To assemble the components of a given electrical circuit.
To study the variation in potential drop with length of a wire for a steady current.

To draw the diagram of a given open circuit comprising at least a battery, resistor/rheostat, key, ammeter and voltmeter. Mark the components that are not connected in proper order and correct the circuit and also the circuit diagram.
To identify a diode, an LED, a resistor and a capacitor from a mixed collection of such items.
Use of multimeter to see the unidirectional flow of current in case of a diode and an LED and check whether a given electronic component (e.g., diode) is in working order.
To study effect of intensity of light (by varying distance of the source) on an LDR.
To observe refraction and lateral deviation of a beam of light incident obliquely on a glass slab.
To observe diffraction of light due to a thin slit.
To study the nature and size of the image formed by a (i) convex lens, or (ii) concave mirror, on a screen by using a candle and a screen (for different distances of the candle from the lens/mirror).
To obtain a lens combination with the specified focal length by using two lenses from the given set of lenses.

projects.title: To study various factors on which the internal resistance/EMF of a cell depends.

To study the variations in current flowing in a circuit containing an LDR because of a variation in (a) the power of the incandescent lamp, used to 'illuminate' the LDR (keeping all the lamps at a fixed distance). (b) the distance of an incandescent lamp (of fixed power) used to 'illuminate' the LDR.
To find the refractive indices of (a) water (b) oil (transparent) using a plane mirror, an equiconvex lens (made from a glass of known refractive index) and an adjustable object needle.
To investigate the relation between the ratio of (i) output and input voltage and (ii) number of turns in the secondary coil and primary coil of a self-designed transformer.
projects.unit_name: Unit-IV Electromagnetic Induction and Alternating Currents

projects.title: To investigate the dependence of the angle of deviation on the angle of incidence using a hollow prism filled one by one, with different transparent fluids.
To estimate the charge induced on each one of the two identical Styrofoam (or pith) balls suspended in a vertical plane by making use of Coulomb's law.

To study the factor on which the self-inductance of a coil depends by observing the effect of this coil, when put in series with a resistor/(bulb) in a circuit fed up by an A.C. source of adjustable frequency.
projects.unit_name: Unit-IV Electromagnetic Induction and Alternating Currents

projects.title: To study the earth's magnetic field using a compass needle -bar magnet by plotting magnetic field lines and tangent galvanometer.
projects.unit_name: Unit-III Magnetic Effects of Current and Magnetism

Demonstrate understanding of facts and ideas by organizing, comparing, translating, interpreting, giving descriptions, and stating main ideas.
Solve problems to new situations by applying acquired knowledge, facts, techniques and rules in a different way.
Examine/break information; Present/defend opinions; Compile information in new patterns/proposing solutions.

Using a simple pendulum, plot its graph and use it to find the effective length of second's pendulum.

To study the relationship between force of limiting friction and normal reaction and to find the co- efficient of friction between a block and a horizontal surface.

To determine Young's modulus of elasticity of the material of a given wire.
To find the force constant of a helical spring by plotting a graph between load and extension.
To study the variation in volume with pressure for a sample of air at constant temperature by plotting graphs between P and V, and between P and 1/V.

To study the relation between frequency and length of a given wire under constant tension using sonometer.

To determine resistivity of two / three wires by plotting a graph for potential difference versus current.

To convert the given galvanometer (of known resistance and figure of merit) into a voltmeter of desired range and to verify the same OR To convert the given galvanometer (of known resistance and figure of merit) into an ammeter of desired range and to verify the same.

To find the value of v for different values of u in case of a concave mirror and to find the focal length.

To determine refractive index of a glass slab using a travelling microscope.

To find the refractive index of a liquid using a concave mirror and a plane mirror.
To draw the I-V characteristic curve for a p-n junction diode in forward and reverse bias.
To determine the resistance per cm of a given wire by plotting a graph between voltage and current.
To verify the laws of combination (series/parallel combination) of resistances by Ohm's law.
To find the resistance of a given wire / standard resistor using a meter bridge.
To determine the resistance of a galvanometer by half deflection method.
To identify a resistor, capacitor, inductor and diode from a mixed collection of such items.

To observe the difference between (i) a convex lens and a concave lens (ii) a convex mirror and a concave mirror and to estimate the likely difference between the power of two given convex /concave lenses.

question_paper_design.description: Exhibit memory of previously learned material by recalling facts, terms, basic concepts, and answers.
Demonstrate understanding of facts and ideas by organizing, comparing, translating, interpreting, giving descriptions, and stating main ideas.
Solve problems to new situations by applying acquired knowledge, facts, techniques and rules in a different way.
Examine/break information; Present/defend opinions; Compile information in new patterns/proposing solutions.

bloom_levels.description: Examine/break info, defend opinions, compile in new patterns

internal_assessment.description: Record of at least 8 Experiments and 6 Activities

project_assessment.description: Report of the project carried out by students

project_assessment.note: Included in practical assessment total (5 marks for viva)

competency_groups.title: Physical World and Measurement
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to measurement systems, SI units, significant figures, uncertainty determination, dimensional analysis, and applications of physical quantities.
competencies: Identify need for measurement and distinguish between fundamental and derived units within the SI system.
Apply rules for significant figures and determine uncertainty in experimental results.
Perform dimensional analysis to verify equations and derive relationships among physical quantities.

competency_groups.title: Kinematics - Linear Motion
competency_groups.description: Competencies involving frame of reference, motion description using calculus concepts (differentiation/integration), velocity-time graphs, and uniformly accelerated motion.
competencies: Describe motion in a straight line using elementary differentiation and integration techniques.
Interpret position-time and velocity-time graphs to analyze uniform and non-uniform motion.
Derive and apply relations for uniformly accelerated motion graphically and through calculus.

competency_groups.title: Vector Algebra and Plane Motion
competency_groups.description: Competencies covering vector operations, resolution of vectors, scalar/vector products, projectile motion, and uniform circular motion.
competencies: Perform addition, subtraction, multiplication by scalars, and resolve vectors into rectangular components in a plane.
Calculate scalar and vector products to solve physical problems involving directionality.
Analyze cases of uniform velocity and acceleration including projectile motion and uniform circular motion.

competency_groups.title: Dynamics - Laws of Motion
competency_groups.description: Competencies focusing on Newton's laws, momentum conservation, impulse, friction types, and dynamics of circular motion.
competencies: Explain intuitive concepts of force, inertia, and apply Newton's three laws to various scenarios.
Apply the law of conservation of linear momentum and analyze collisions using impulse-momentum theorem.
Analyze equilibrium conditions for concurrent forces and dynamics involving static/kinetic friction.

competency_groups.title: Work, Energy, and Power
competency_groups.description: Competencies involving work done by constant/variable forces, kinetic/potential energy concepts, conservation laws, collisions, and power.
competencies: Calculate work done by constant and variable forces and apply the work-energy theorem.
Distinguish between conservative and non-conservative forces and analyze potential energy of springs.
Analyze elastic and inelastic collisions in one and two dimensions using conservation principles.

competency_groups.title: Rotational Motion Dynamics
competency_groups.description: Competencies covering center of mass, torque, angular momentum conservation, rigid body equilibrium, moment of inertia, and rotational equations.
competencies: Determine the center of mass for particle systems and rigid bodies like uniform rods.
Apply laws of conservation of linear and angular momentum to solve dynamics problems.
Calculate torque, moment of inertia, and apply equations of rotational motion comparing them with linear counterparts.
competency_groups.unit_name: Motion of System of Particles and Rigid Body
competency_groups.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

competency_groups.title: Gravitation
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to Kepler's laws, universal gravitation, variation of gravity, gravitational potential/energy, orbital mechanics, and escape speed.
competencies: Apply Kepler's laws and Newton's law of universal gravitation to planetary motion problems.
Analyze the variation of acceleration due to gravity with altitude and depth.
Calculate gravitational potential energy, escape speed, and orbital velocity for satellites.

competency_groups.title: Mechanical Properties of Solids
competency_groups.description: Competencies involving elasticity, stress-strain relationships, Hooke's law, moduli (Young's, Bulk, Shear), Poisson's ratio, and elastic energy.
competencies: Interpret stress-strain curves to identify material properties like Young's modulus and bulk modulus.
Apply Hooke's Law qualitatively and quantitatively to analyze deformation in solids.
Calculate elastic potential energy stored in deformed materials.

competency_groups.title: Fluid Mechanics and Surface Phenomena
competency_groups.description: Competencies covering fluid pressure, Pascal's law, viscosity, Bernoulli's theorem, surface tension, capillarity, and flow regimes.
competencies: Apply Pascal's Law to hydraulic systems and analyze pressure variation in fluids under gravity.
Use Stokes' Law and terminal velocity concepts to study viscous drag on spherical bodies.
Explain phenomena like Bernoulli's theorem, surface tension, capillary rise, and excess pressure across curved surfaces.

competency_groups.title: Thermal Properties of Matter
competency_groups.description: Competencies involving heat, temperature, thermal expansion, specific heats, latent heat, modes of heat transfer, and blackbody radiation laws.
competencies: Differentiate between heat and temperature and calculate calorimetric quantities including specific heats (Cp, Cv).
Analyze phase changes using latent heat concepts and anomalous expansion of water.
Apply conduction, convection, and radiation principles to solve thermal equilibrium problems.

competency_groups.title: Thermodynamics
competency_groups.description: Competencies covering thermal equilibrium, laws of thermodynamics (0th to 2nd), state variables, and processes like isothermal/adiabatic.
competencies: Define temperature using the zeroth law and distinguish between heat, work, and internal energy.
Apply the first law of thermodynamics to various cyclic and non-cyclic processes (isothermal, adiabatic).
Explain the second law of thermodynamics regarding entropy and efficiency of engines.

competency_groups.title: Kinetic Theory of Gases
competency_groups.description: Competencies involving the equation of state, kinetic assumptions, pressure derivation, temperature interpretation, degrees of freedom, and mean free path.
competencies: Derive or apply the ideal gas law from kinetic theory assumptions regarding molecular motion.
Interpret temperature as a measure of average kinetic energy (rms speed) of molecules.
Apply the law of equipartition of energy to specific heat capacities and understand mean free path concepts.
competency_groups.unit_name: Behaviour of Perfect Gases and Kinetic Theory of Gases

competency_groups.title: Oscillations (SHM)
competency_groups.description: Competencies covering periodic motion, simple harmonic motion equations, energy in SHM, and analysis of loaded springs and pendulums.
competencies: Identify conditions for Simple Harmonic Motion (S.H.M) and derive its equation from restoring force principles.
Analyze the relationship between uniform circular motion and S.H.M including phase concepts.
Calculate time periods of loaded springs and simple pendulums using energy or kinematic methods.

competency_groups.title: Wave Motion and Superposition
competency_groups.description: Competencies involving wave types, speed, superposition principle, standing waves, beats, reflection, and interference phenomena.
competencies: Distinguish between transverse and longitudinal waves and describe progressive wave characteristics.
Apply the principle of superposition to explain standing waves in strings and organ pipes (harmonics).
Analyze beat formation due to frequency differences and reflection properties of waves.

competency_groups.title: Electrostatics - Charges and Fields
competency_groups.description: Competencies covering charge conservation, Coulomb's law, superposition principle, electric fields of various distributions, dipoles, and Gauss's theorem.
competencies: Apply Coulomb's Law and the superposition principle to calculate forces between multiple charges.
Determine electric field intensity due to point charges, dipoles, continuous distributions (wire, sheet, shell).
Utilize Gauss's Theorem to derive fields for symmetric charge configurations.

competency_groups.title: Electrostatic Potential and Capacitance
competency_groups.description: Competencies involving electric potential, equipotential surfaces, energy of charge systems, dielectrics, capacitors (series/parallel), and stored energy.
competencies: Calculate electric potential and potential difference due to point charges, dipoles, and systems of charges.
Analyze the behavior of conductors, insulators, and dielectrics in electrostatic fields including polarization effects.
Compute equivalent capacitance for series/parallel combinations and energy stored in capacitors.

competency_groups.title: Current Electricity
competency_groups.description: Competencies covering drift velocity, Ohm's law, resistivity/conductivity, temperature dependence, internal resistance, Kirchhoff's rules, Wheatstone bridge.
competencies: Relate electric current to drift velocity and mobility; analyze V-I characteristics (linear/non-linear).
Apply Ohm's Law and concepts of resistivity/conductivity including temperature effects on resistance.
Solve complex circuits using Kirchhoff's rules, internal resistance analysis, and Wheatstone bridge principles.

competency_groups.title: Magnetic Effects of Current - Moving Charges
competency_groups.description: Competencies involving magnetic fields, Biot-Savart law, Ampere's law, forces on charges/conductors, torque on loops, and moving coil galvanometer.
competencies: Apply Biot-Savart Law to find magnetic fields due to current elements (e.g., circular loop).
Use Ampere's Circuital Law for symmetric conductors like infinite wires and solenoids.
Analyze forces on moving charges/current-carrying conductors in uniform magnetic fields.
competency_groups.unit_name: Unit-III Magnetic Effects of Current and Magnetism

competency_groups.title: Magnetism and Matter
competency_groups.description: Competencies covering bar magnets, magnetic dipoles, torque on dipoles, field lines, classification of materials (para/dia/ferro), and temperature effects.
competencies: Model a bar magnet as an equivalent solenoid and calculate fields along its axis/perpendicular bisector.
Classify magnetic materials based on their response to external fields and explain magnetization processes.
Analyze the effect of temperature on magnetic properties of different substances.
competency_groups.unit_name: Unit-III Magnetic Effects of Current and Magnetism

competency_groups.title: Electromagnetic Induction
competency_groups.description: Competencies involving Faraday's laws, Lenz's law, induced EMF/current, self-induction, mutual induction.
competencies: Apply Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction to calculate induced EMF and current direction (Lenz's Law).
Explain the concepts of self-induction and mutual induction in coupled circuits.
competency_groups.unit_name: Unit-IV Electromagnetic Induction and Alternating Currents

competency_groups.title: Alternating Current (AC)
competency_groups.description: Competencies covering AC parameters, reactance/impedance, LCR circuits, resonance, power factor, wattless current, generators, and transformers.
competencies: Distinguish between peak/RMS values of AC voltage/current and calculate reactance and impedance in RLC series circuits.
Analyze resonance conditions in AC circuits and the concept of power factor/wattless current.
Explain the working principles of AC generators and transformers.
competency_groups.unit_name: Unit-IV Electromagnetic Induction and Alternating Currents

competency_groups.title: Electromagnetic Waves
competency_groups.description: Competencies involving displacement current, wave characteristics (transverse nature), electromagnetic spectrum types and uses.
competencies: Understand the concept of displacement current as a source of magnetic fields in changing electric fields.
Describe the transverse nature of EM waves and their propagation characteristics.

competency_groups.title: Ray Optics - Reflection and Refraction
competency_groups.description: Competencies covering reflection laws, spherical mirrors (formula), refraction at surfaces/lenses (thin lens formula, magnification), prisms.
competencies: Apply mirror formulas and sign conventions to analyze image formation by spherical mirrors.
Use thin lens formulas and lens maker's equation for single lenses and combinations in contact.

competency_groups.title: Optical Instruments and Wave Optics
competency_groups.description: Competencies involving microscopes/telescopes, Huygens' principle, interference (Young's), diffraction.
competencies: Calculate magnifying powers of optical instruments like microscopes and telescopes.
Apply Huygen's Principle to derive laws of reflection/refraction using wave fronts.

competency_groups.title: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
competency_groups.description: Competencies covering photoelectric effect, Einstein's equation, particle nature of light, matter waves (de-Broglie).
competencies: Explain the experimental observations of the photoelectric effect using Einstein's photoelectric equation.
Apply de Broglie relation to calculate wavelength associated with moving particles.
competency_groups.unit_name: Unit–VII Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
competency_groups.chapter_name: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

competency_groups.title: Atomic Structure (Rutherford & Bohr)
competency_groups.description: Competencies involving alpha scattering, Rutherford model limitations, Bohr's postulates, hydrogen spectrum.
competencies: Interpret the results of the alpha-particle scattering experiment leading to the nuclear model of the atom.
Apply Bohr's quantization conditions to derive expressions for radius and energy levels in hydrogen.

competency_groups.title: Nuclear Physics
competency_groups.description: Competencies covering nuclear composition, mass-energy relation (E=mc^2), binding energy curve, fission, and fusion.
competencies: Explain the concept of mass defect and calculate binding energy per nucleon for various nuclei.

competency_groups.title: Semiconductor Electronics
competency_groups.description: Competencies involving energy bands, intrinsic/extrinsic semiconductors (p/n type), p-n junction diode characteristics and rectification.
competencies: Distinguish between conductors, insulators, and semiconductors based on energy band theory.

marks_distribution: Class XI/XII Theory: Max Marks 70, Time 3 hours
Class XI/XII Practical: Max Marks 30, Time 2-3 hours (Visually Impaired: 2 hours)

question_paper_design.description: Exhibit memory of previously learned material by recalling facts, terms, basic concepts, and answers.
Demonstrate understanding of facts and ideas by organizing, comparing, translating, interpreting, giving descriptions, and stating main ideas.
Solve problems to new situations by applying acquired knowledge, facts, techniques and rules in a different way.
Examine/break information; Present/defend opinions; Compile information in new patterns/proposing solutions.

bloom_levels.description: Examine/break info, defend opinions, compile in new patterns

internal_assessment.description: Record of at least 8 Experiments and 6 Activities

project_assessment.description: Report of the project carried out by students

project_assessment.note: Included in practical assessment total (5 marks for viva)

outcomes: Identify need for measurement and distinguish between fundamental and derived units within the SI system.
Apply rules for significant figures and determine uncertainty in experimental results.
Perform dimensional analysis to verify equations and derive relationships among physical quantities.
Describe motion in a straight line using elementary differentiation and integration techniques.
Interpret position-time and velocity-time graphs to analyze uniform and non-uniform motion.
Derive and apply relations for uniformly accelerated motion graphically and through calculus.

learning_outcomes.topic: Vector Algebra and Plane Motion Dynamics
outcomes: Perform addition, subtraction, multiplication by scalars, and resolve vectors into rectangular components in a plane.
Calculate scalar and vector products to solve physical problems involving directionality.
Analyze cases of uniform velocity and acceleration including projectile motion and uniform circular motion.
Explain intuitive concepts of force, inertia, and apply Newton's three laws to various scenarios.
Apply the law of conservation of linear momentum and analyze collisions using impulse-momentum theorem.
Analyze equilibrium conditions for concurrent forces and dynamics involving static/kinetic friction.
Calculate work done by constant and variable forces and apply the work-energy theorem.
Distinguish between conservative and non-conservative forces and analyze potential energy of springs.
Analyze elastic and inelastic collisions in one and two dimensions using conservation principles.

learning_outcomes.unit_name: Motion of System of Particles and Rigid Body
learning_outcomes.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
outcomes: Determine the center of mass for particle systems and rigid bodies like uniform rods.
Apply laws of conservation of linear and angular momentum to solve dynamics problems.
Calculate torque, moment of inertia, and apply equations of rotational motion comparing them with linear counterparts.
Apply Kepler's laws and Newton's law of universal gravitation to planetary motion problems.
Analyze the variation of acceleration due to gravity with altitude and depth.
Calculate gravitational potential energy, escape speed, and orbital velocity for satellites.
Interpret stress-strain curves to identify material properties like Young's modulus and bulk modulus.
Apply Hooke's Law qualitatively and quantitatively to analyze deformation in solids.
Calculate elastic potential energy stored in deformed materials.
Apply Pascal's Law to hydraulic systems and analyze pressure variation in fluids under gravity.
Use Stokes' Law and terminal velocity concepts to study viscous drag on spherical bodies.
Explain phenomena like Bernoulli's theorem, surface tension, capillary rise, and excess pressure across curved surfaces.
Differentiate between heat and temperature and calculate calorimetric quantities including specific heats (Cp, Cv).
Analyze phase changes using latent heat concepts and anomalous expansion of water.
Apply conduction, convection, and radiation principles to solve thermal equilibrium problems.
Define temperature using the zeroth law and distinguish between heat, work, and internal energy.
Apply the first law of thermodynamics to various cyclic and non-cyclic processes (isothermal, adiabatic).
Explain the second law of thermodynamics regarding entropy and efficiency of engines.

learning_outcomes.unit_name: Behaviour of Perfect Gases and Kinetic Theory of Gases
outcomes: Derive or apply the ideal gas law from kinetic theory assumptions regarding molecular motion.
Interpret temperature as a measure of average kinetic energy (rms speed) of molecules.
Apply the law of equipartition of energy to specific heat capacities and understand mean free path concepts.
Identify conditions for Simple Harmonic Motion (S.H.M) and derive its equation from restoring force principles.
Analyze the relationship between uniform circular motion and S.H.M including phase concepts.
Calculate time periods of loaded springs and simple pendulums using energy or kinematic methods.
Distinguish between transverse and longitudinal waves and describe progressive wave characteristics.
Apply the principle of superposition to explain standing waves in strings and organ pipes (harmonics).
Analyze beat formation due to frequency differences and reflection properties of waves.
Apply Coulomb's Law and the superposition principle to calculate forces between multiple charges.
Determine electric field intensity due to point charges, dipoles, continuous distributions (wire, sheet, shell).
Utilize Gauss's Theorem to derive fields for symmetric charge configurations.
Calculate electric potential and potential difference due to point charges, dipoles, and systems of charges.
Analyze the behavior of conductors, insulators, and dielectrics in electrostatic fields including polarization effects.
Compute equivalent capacitance for series/parallel combinations and energy stored in capacitors.
Relate electric current to drift velocity and mobility; analyze V-I characteristics (linear/non-linear).
Apply Ohm's Law and concepts of resistivity/conductivity including temperature effects on resistance.
Solve complex circuits using Kirchhoff's rules, internal resistance analysis, and Wheatstone bridge principles.

learning_outcomes.unit_name: Unit-III Magnetic Effects of Current and Magnetism
learning_outcomes.topic: Magnetic Effects of Current - Moving Charges
outcomes: Apply Biot-Savart Law to find magnetic fields due to current elements (e.g., circular loop).
Use Ampere's Circuital Law for symmetric conductors like infinite wires and solenoids.
Analyze forces on moving charges/current-carrying conductors in uniform magnetic fields.

learning_outcomes.unit_name: Unit-III Magnetic Effects of Current and Magnetism
outcomes: Model a bar magnet as an equivalent solenoid and calculate fields along its axis/perpendicular bisector.
Classify magnetic materials based on their response to external fields and explain magnetization processes.
Analyze the effect of temperature on magnetic properties of different substances.

learning_outcomes.unit_name: Unit-IV Electromagnetic Induction and Alternating Currents
outcomes: Apply Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction to calculate induced EMF and current direction (Lenz's Law).
Explain the concepts of self-induction and mutual induction in coupled circuits.

learning_outcomes.unit_name: Unit-IV Electromagnetic Induction and Alternating Currents
outcomes: Distinguish between peak/RMS values of AC voltage/current and calculate reactance and impedance in RLC series circuits.
Analyze resonance conditions in AC circuits and the concept of power factor/wattless current.
Explain the working principles of AC generators and transformers.
Understand the concept of displacement current as a source of magnetic fields in changing electric fields.
Describe the transverse nature of EM waves and their propagation characteristics.
Apply mirror formulas and sign conventions to analyze image formation by spherical mirrors.
Use thin lens formulas and lens maker's equation for single lenses and combinations in contact.
Calculate magnifying powers of optical instruments like microscopes and telescopes.
Apply Huygen's Principle to derive laws of reflection/refraction using wave fronts.

learning_outcomes.unit_name: Unit–VII Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
learning_outcomes.chapter_name: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
learning_outcomes.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
outcomes: Explain the experimental observations of the photoelectric effect using Einstein's photoelectric equation.
Apply de Broglie relation to calculate wavelength associated with moving particles.
Interpret the results of the alpha-particle scattering experiment leading to the nuclear model of the atom.
Apply Bohr's quantization conditions to derive expressions for radius and energy levels in hydrogen.
Explain the concept of mass defect and calculate binding energy per nucleon for various nuclei.
Distinguish between conductors, insulators, and semiconductors based on energy band theory.

To observe the decrease in pressure with increase in velocity of a fluid.
To measure the resistance and impedance of an inductor with or without iron core.

projects.title: To study various factors on which the internal resistance/EMF of a cell depends.

To study the variations in current flowing in a circuit containing an LDR because of a variation in (a) the power of the incandescent lamp, used to 'illuminate' the LDR (keeping all the lamps at a fixed distance). To find the refractive indices of (a) water (b) oil (transparent) using a plane mirror, an equiconvex lens (made from a glass of known refractive index) and an adjustable object needle.
To investigate the relation between the ratio of (i) output and input voltage and (ii) number of turns in the secondary coil and primary coil of a self-designed transformer.

To study the factor on which the self-inductance of a coil depends by observing the effect of this coil, when put in series with a resistor/(bulb) in a circuit fed up by an A.C. source of adjustable frequency.
